request.question: California Suite is a 1976 play by an american playwright born in what year?

title: Anthony Clarvoe
Edward Locke
London Suite (play)
Jen Silverman
Vinegar Tom
California Suite
The Vietnamization of New Jersey
Eden (Steve Carter play)
California Suite (film)
Neil Simon
sentences: Anthony Clarvoe is an American playwright born in 1958.
Edward Locke (1869–1945) was an American playwright born in England.
London Suite is a play by Neil Simon, consisting of four one-act plays.
"London Suite" also was a 1996 television movie.
It is in a similar style to Simon's earlier works "Plaza Suite" and "California Suite".
Jen Silverman is an American playwright born in the United States.
She grew up living and traveling in Scandinavia, Asia, and Europe as well as the United States.
She completed a BA in comparative literature at Brown University, a MFA in playwriting at the University of Iowa, and also studied at Juilliard.
Vinegar Tom is the title of a 1976 play by the British playwright Caryl Churchill.
The play examines gender and power relationships through the lens of 17th century witchcraft trials in England.
The script employs features of the epic theater associated with German playwright Bertolt Brecht, particularly the realistic use of songs.

The play's title comes from the name of one character's pet cat, supposed to be her familiar spirit, likely inspired by the supposed imp of one Elizabeth Clarke, a woman tried and executed for witchcraft in Essex in 1645.

The play was inspired by the Women's Rights Act in 1970 and explored the thought that women were treated unequally to men in England, both at the time in which the play takes place, and the time in which the play was written.
California Suite is a 1976 play by Neil Simon.

Similar in structure to his earlier "Plaza Suite", the comedy is composed of four playlets set in Suite 203-04, which consists of a living room and an adjoining bedroom with an ensuite bath, in The Beverly Hills Hotel.
The Vietnamization of New Jersey (subtitled "A American Tragedy" ["sic"] ) is a 1976 play written by American playwright Christopher Durang.
Eden is a 1976 play by American playwright Steve Carter.
Set in the 1920s, it is the first of Carter's Caribbean trilogy.
"Eden" explores intra-racial conflicts between recent immigrants from the Caribbean and the African-American population.
The West Coast premiere of this critically acclaimed play received five Los Angeles Drama Critics Circle Awards.
California Suite is a 1978 American comedy film directed by Herbert Ross.
The screenplay by Neil Simon is based on his play of the same name.
Similar to his earlier "Plaza Suite", the film focuses on the dilemmas of guests staying in a suite in a luxury hotel.
Maggie Smith won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress for her performance in the film.
Marvin Neil Simon (born July 4, 1927) is an American playwright, screenwriter and author.
He has written more than thirty plays and nearly the same number of movie screenplays, mostly adaptations of his plays.
He has received more combined Oscar and Tony nominations than any other writer.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here. Emit exactly one field named `summary`.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A one-line header naming the primary topic(s) identified in the passages.
- 1–4 short lines enumerating concrete facts directly supported by the passages. Extract entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Do not add or infer facts not present in the passages.
- An explicit statement that names the missing bridge fact or relation needed for the next hop (express in abstract role terms, e.g., "missing bridge fact: [relation] between [candidate entity] and [target attribute]"). If passages supply candidate bridge entities but do not confirm the required relation, label them as "possible bridge entity" and state what is unverified for each.
- 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (highest priority first). Each directive must be concrete and section-aware: specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Early life", "History"), or tight search queries/keywords. Prefer section-level targets and tight searches over broad suggestions.

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` compact: one-line header plus a total of 1–4 brief lines (sentences or bullet lines) covering the factual spans, the missing bridge fact, and the prioritized retrieval directives.
- Preserve exact factual spans from the passages; when quoting, keep quotes very short (<= ~15 words) and only if verbatim in the passages.
- Do not assert a final answer or resolve facts here. If the passages do fully and unambiguously contain the required relation, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but still do not produce a final answer.
- If multiple candidate entities or variant names appear, enumerate the top 1–2 candidates and mark the name as ambiguous, stating what is unverified for each.
- Do not invent facts, hypothesize, or add extraneous context not grounded in the provided passages.

Output expectation: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary as specified above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Priority rules for ambiguity and disambiguation:
- If summary_1 lists multiple candidate anchors or otherwise signals ambiguity, emit a single connector-style query that concatenates the prioritized anchor tokens (use the order or priority visible in summary_1) and then append exactly one short attribute token or a visible year token for disambiguation.
- If summary_1 contains a comma-separated list of individuals and the missing evidence concerns a member, prefer the earliest-listed individual token paired with the visible group token and append the single attribute token member.
- If the question contains a visible year token that disambiguates a title, include that year token before the attribute token in the query.
- If none of the above applies, emit a single-anchor query using the most relevant visible token and at most one short attribute token when the question implies an attribute.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (prefer the form used in the context when there is a name-variant conflict) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Follow these additional concise operational rules when integrating evidence:
- Prefer the context-preferred name form for the preserved first (answer) sentence; when choosing a canonical name for supporting sentences, prefer the context form if there is a name-variant conflict across inputs.
- If a passage explicitly contradicts a preserved context sentence, replace the preserved sentence with a passage-supported first sentence or note the contradiction concisely in that first sentence.
- For each supporting sentence, start with the chosen canonical name and include only attributes explicitly present in the provided passages; if the passages do not assert a requested status or attribute, state that absence briefly rather than inferring the attribute.
- Do not add fields, requests for more retrieval, or change the input/output interface. Keep the `summary` to 1–3 sentences exactly and avoid extra commentary or formatting.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add any new facts or external information.
- First run a mandatory binary detection: determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to extraction.

Conservative extractive tie-breaking (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- When a contiguous longer verbatim phrase in either summary strictly contains a shorter candidate (adds adjacent words that are part of the name or label), emit the longest contiguous verbatim phrase exactly as it appears in the summaries.
- For personal names where one verbatim form is a substring of another, prefer the longest verbatim personal-name span found in either summary; if multiple distinct spans tie for length, prefer the form from `summary_2`.
- If either summary presents an alternate token parenthetically immediately adjacent to a name/place (e.g., canonical short form in parentheses), emit that parenthetical token exactly as shown. If both summaries present parenthetical tokens, prefer the one in `summary_2`.
- If the summaries do not explicitly state the requested relation but exactly one candidate entity of the relevant role appears verbatim across the summaries and no summary contradicts the attribution, emit that single verbatim entity. If multiple distinct candidates appear or there is any contradiction, do not apply this rule and return the unresolved outcome.
- Prefer wording from `summary_2` only as a tie-breaker, except when the longest-contiguous-span or parenthetical-token rules require choosing a longer or parenthetical form present in `summary_1`.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest contiguous verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind"). If multiple adjacent phrases appear, prefer the longest contiguous phrase unless that creates contradiction.
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

Use only the provided summaries as evidence and follow the conservative tie-breaking rules above when extracting the final minimal verbatim span.

response.hop1_query: California Suite is a 1976 play by an american playwright born in what year?
hop1_docs: California Suite | California Suite is a 1976 play by Neil Simon.  Similar in structure to his earlier "Plaza Suite", the comedy is composed of four playlets set in Suite 203-04, which consists of a living room and an adjoining bedroom with an ensuite bath, in The Beverly Hills Hotel.

J. California Cooper | Joan Cooper (November 10, 1931, Berkeley, California – September 20, 2014, Seattle, Washington), known by her pen name, J. California Cooper, was an American playwright and author.  She wrote 17 plays and was named Black Playwright of the Year in 1978 for her play "Strangers".

California Suite (film) | California Suite is a 1978 American comedy film directed by Herbert Ross.  The screenplay by Neil Simon is based on his play of the same name.  Similar to his earlier "Plaza Suite", the film focuses on the dilemmas of guests staying in a suite in a luxury hotel.  Maggie Smith won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress for her performance in the film.
What I Did Last Summer | What I Did Last Summer is a play by the American playwright A.R. Gurney.
Donkeys' Years | Donkeys' Years is a play by English playwright Michael Frayn that premiered at the Globe Theatre, London, in 1976.

Frederick J. Jackson | Frederick J. Jackson (September 21, 1886 – May 22, 1953) was an American author, playwright and screenwriter.  He wrote for over 50 films between 1912 and 1946.  Over a forty-year span, a dozen of his plays were produced on Broadway.  Several of his plays were turned into films, including "The Bishop Misbehaves".  He was born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania and died in Hollywood, California.

David Mamet | David Alan Mamet ( ; born November 30, 1947) is an American playwright, essayist, screenwriter, and film director.  As a playwright, Mamet has won a Pulitzer Prize and received Tony nominations for "Glengarry Glen Ross" (1984) and "Speed-the-Plow" (1988).  Mamet first gained acclaim for a trio of off-Broadway plays in 1976, "The Duck Variations," "Sexual Perversity in Chicago," and "American Buffalo."  His play "Race" opened on Broadway on December 6, 2009, and his play "The Penitent" previewed off-Broadway on February 8, 2017.
response.summary_1:
California Suite / Neil Simon
- "California Suite is a 1976 play by Neil Simon." (passage 1)

- Next-hop (backup): search query "Neil Simon birth year" or fetch authoritative biographies/obituaries (news outlets, Britannica).
hop2_docs: Mel Tolkin | Mel Tolkin, né Shmuel Tolchinsky(August 3, 1913 – November 26, 2007), was a television comedy writer best known as head writer of the seminal live TV sketch comedy series "Your Show of Shows" (NBC, 1950–1954) during the Golden Age of Television.  There he presided over a storied staff that at times included Mel Brooks, Neil Simon, Danny Simon, and Larry Gelbart.  The writers' room inspired the film "My Favorite Year" (1982), produced by Brooks, and the Broadway play "Laughter on the 23rd Floor" (1993), written by Neil Simon.
Simon Neil | Simon Alexander Neil (born 31 August 1979) is a Scottish vocalist, guitarist, and songwriter.

Neil Simon's I Ought to Be in Pictures | Neil Simon's I Ought to Be in Pictures (also known simply as I Ought to Be in Pictures) is a 1982 American comedy-drama film directed by Herbert Ross and based on Neil Simon's play of the same name.  The film stars Walter Matthau, Ann-Margret, and Dinah Manoff (the only cast member to reprise her Broadway role in the film).  Other actors who have supporting roles are Lance Guest, Eugene Butler, David Faustino, Martin Ferrero and Michael Dudikoff.

Neil Simon Theatre | The Neil Simon Theatre, formerly the Alvin Theatre, is a Broadway venue built in 1927 and located at 250 West 52nd Street in the Theater District of Midtown Manhattan, New York City.

Last of the Red Hot Lovers (film) | Neil Simon's Last of the Red Hot Lovers is a 1972 comedy film based on Neil Simon's play of the same name.  Alan Arkin, Sally Kellerman, Paula Prentiss and Renée Taylor star in it.

Simon of Tournai | Simon of Tournai (c. 1130–1201) was a professor at the University of Paris in the late twelfth century.  His date of birth is uncertain, but he was teaching before 1184, as he signed a document at the same time as Gerard de Pucelle, the Bishop of Coventry, who died that year.

Emanuel Azenberg | Emanuel "Manny" Azenberg (born January 22, 1934) is an American theatre producer and general manager whose professional relationship with playwright Neil Simon spans thirty-three years.

Last of the Red Hot Lovers (film)
